items.title: 3 Reasons Why Friends Are Better at Strategy than Business Executives
items.author: Ágoston Török
items.description:
Because purpose beats process — and most companies get that backwards.

Imagine the following: Albert, Tobias, Günther and Leni are highschool friends. Today, they are all in their thirties and live in different parts of Europe, but this year they decided to visit the Oktoberfest together. They planed it, executed it and nailed it. No strategy deck. No MBA. No management consulting.

Now imagine the same four as executives, heading different departments of the same company planning next year. Dozens of slides. Yet misalignment in execution. So:

Why do people who’ve never studied strategy outperform people who get paid to do it?

The thesis I’m making here is that friends align through a shared purpose. Executives align through a shared process. The first creates strategy. The second creates bureaucracy.

Reason #1 — Start with the purpose

Simon Sinek sketched the concept of the golden circle in his book Start with why. The core tennet there is that leaders should start with explaining their purpose (“Why”) before explaining “How” they want to reach that and “What” the team have to do. This is a model well known to executives too, what is less known how much it’s not the presentation but the understanding that matters. For the friends well before the Oktoberfest they had a shared why: Friendship brings joy. The Oktoberfest was only part of the how: Spend time together there. And the organizational aspects all derive from that: Some may need to fly, others can take a train, others will drive and in our example, Leni decided to bike from her hometown.

In companies, ‘why’ turns into quarterly OKRs. ‘How’ becomes frameworks. ‘What’ becomes KPIs. Everyone’s busy, no one’s aligned. See, on a purpose level a “successful next year” means different things for the CFO and the CTO. The CFO is going to think along words such as revenue, profit or cost saves, while the CTO is going to think along scaling technology, new disruptions, connecting components. Even their incentives are biased so they think like this, few CFO gets bonus based on key patents filed. This is where a CEO will come to picture and makes either a deal where everyone’s incentives are part of the strategy or where they make a “deal” with the executive whose own incentives are going to suffer so the company makes a great year.

Reason #2 — Shared mental representation

When the four friends plan the trip, they probably have a totally different mental model from Fig 1. While Fig 1. displays the geographical relations, it says very little about two key components of the “What”: the effort and the timing. The mental model of the friends is something more alike (but definitely not exactly :D) the below Wardley-Torok map:

Look at how different this representation is from the above conventional map. All of a sudden the story is not dominated by Günther’s travel from London. Instead we start to see much better that Tobias, Günther and Albert will need to coordinate so Albert can pick the other two up with his car. Also, it will become clear that actually Leni - who is otherwise the closest to the venue - chose the most risky way of travel and the other three will pray so she arrives safely.

If you can’t see the same landscape, you’re not on the same journey.

It’s also clear that different purposes (i.e. “Whys) yield different representations of the “What”. Imagine that our executives are doing the planning and one of them — Günther, Chief Marketing Officer — wants to show how important his contribution to the success is. Which kind of representation would he use? The one where his journey dominates or the one where he’s seemingly playing only a supporting role?

Executives rarely share the same map. Marketing sees brand reach, finance sees runway, product sees roadmap. Different terrains, different coordinates, different executions.

Reason #3 — Zooming in and out

Once you have a shared and aligned purpose, it’s much easier to zoom in and out along the golden circle dimension. Let’s say Tobias starts to worry that it’s might be raining on one of the days. That on our Wardley-Torok map is a threat that risks the goal, that is enjoying the Oktoberfest. However, it’s very likely that one of the others are going to zoom out and say “but the main goal is to have fun together” — from here refining the How and What is much easier — “so we can get umbrellas or raincoat or even find a place that we can approach with minimal exposure to the rain.”

When friends zoom out, they adapt. When executives can’t, they escalate — because hierarchy replaces perspective.

This kind of democratic process rarely works well in the executives setting. Often you need either a process or more often a CEO who owns the Why-How-What picture ultimately and can keep the discussion focused. This is no easy job, so much so that many CEOs cannot effectively cope with this task. This results in something I call PowerPoint cartography: lots of slides and complex figures with zero relation to the actual terrain where the business operates.

Without clear knowledge of the different levels of golden circle, you end with a representation (typically a long document) that contains everything… and it’s opposite as well. These documents are useful for

  • (1) making a strategy inclusive of everyone’s own angle.
  • (2) divert blame from management because ___ (insert failed component of success) was also part of strategy and it was the people who didn’t understand it well.

And these documents are definitely cannot be used for

  • (3) executing on a common strategy together

This is because there is a finite envelope of attention, so people have a limited bandwidth of what they can process as information. Heck, even the above Wardley-Torok maps can easily breach that limit. A painful lesson that I learned many times over.

Level Friends (Purpose-driven) Executives (Process-driven) Reason #1 Start with “why” Start with frameworks and KPIs Reason #2 Shared mental model (“we see the same map”) Shared model of procedure (“we use the same template”) Reason #3 Zoom naturally between purpose and action Zoom mechanically between slide levels

Purpose without illusion

Friends succeed because they share a clear purpose.
Now ask yourself: does your company have one — or just a plan that looks like one?

When people truly share a purpose, everything else — priorities, trade-offs, even setbacks — align naturally. That shared understanding becomes your biggest advantage.

If it is not there, don’t pretend it is. Purpose at the cultural level is extremely hard to retrofit. Only a rare few leaders manage it. Most companies don’t really share a purpose, and that’s fine — someone just has to own it. That person has to keep the ‘why’ alive, align the ‘how’ and ‘what’ around it, and treat friction and small misalignments part of the terrain, not a failure.

A good strategy doesn’t need perfect unity — it needs coherence, solid processes, and a leader who stays anchored while adjusting the course as reality shifts. Authentic belief in the purpose scales better than forced consensus.

3 Reasons Why Friends Are Better at Strategy than Business Executives was originally published in DataDrivenInvestor on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: Ten Patent Cases That Formed History
items.author: Ágoston Török
items.description:
Ten Patent Cases That Shaped History

Beyond legalese, there is a clever system for intellectual property protection

Working together with researchers, engineers, and lawyers on inventions and patents is among my favourite activities in my job. Early on, I had the fortune of learning from some very clever inventors and patent attorneys how a patent is built up and gaining a good understanding of the patenting language. No, I still can’t say I speak legalese but there are inventions, patents and patent dispute cases that I admire for their striking examples of human ingenuity.

The cases we are going to cover:

· 1. Infringement Without a Product: NTP v. Research in Motion
· 2. Function or Fashion? Apple v. Samsung Electronics
· 3. The Fine Line Between Brilliant and Trivial: Amazon v. Barnes & Noble
· 4. The Patent War of Attrition: Nokia vs. Apple
· 5. Pricing Out the Competition: Google Inc. v. Art+Com
· 6. The Longest Process in Instant Photography: Kodak v. Polaroid
· 7. The Local Inventor Bias: Chint vs. Schneider Electric
· 8. Meanwhile in Europe: Panasonic v. Oppo
· 9. Can You Infringe a Patent in Your Own Backyard? Bowman v. Monsanto
· 10. HeLa Cells: Discovery v. Invention

1. Infringement Without a Product: NTP v. Research in Motion

I guess you haven’t heard about NTP, but if you are over 30, you know very Research in Motion (RIM), the producer of BlackBerry phones. If you don’t know what those were, then in short, in the 2000s, every business person was fidgeting with one while they were talking about how important people they were. NTP, though never produced phones, in fact they don’t have any business other than holding and enforcing the patent portfolio of the founder, Thomas Campana. Campana was a smart inventor and a good patent expert; he patented some key technology steps underlying telecommunications and then tried licensing these technologies to big firms like AT&T and RIM.

Since RIM didn’t buy the license, NTP sued them in 2001 for infringing five of their patents covering the push-email technology that was BlackBerry’s unique selling point. The stakes were high because the court could have stopped RIM from using the technology, which would have been a disaster for all 3.5 million users in the US.

An iPhone 4 and a Blackberry Q10 next to each other. While the world was turning towards iPhone at this time, I did everything to get a Q10 for myself. I loved it! Image from Karlis Dambrans

The situation was so severe that the US Department of Defense (another heavy BlackBerry user at the time) also intervened and told the court that stopping the use of technology would cripple national telecommunications. RIM also tried everything; they spent a fortune getting the best lawyers and reasoned that because the servers are in Canada, the US patent law doesn’t apply; but none of these helped.

The resolution was ultimately to settle outside of court in 2006, and RIM agreed to pay NTP $612.5 million for a perpetual license on their patents. This was the largest settlement to date at that point showing how even tech giants are vulnerable to smaller entities holding essential patents.

Companies like NTP are an integral component of the patenting world today. They show that patents protect not only a company’s products or revenue streams but also its inventions. While NTP managed the portfolio of its founder, many similar firms just buy patents from inventors or bankrupt companies. These latter types are often referred to as patent trolls. If you are interested in how effective these companies can be, check out VLSI Technology (LLC, not Inc) and Uniloc USA.

2. Function or Fashion? Apple v. Samsung Electronics

When you hold an Apple or a Samsung phone in your hand, their shape and layout have some characteristics that are unique to the brand e.g. Apple had the circle home key while Samsung had a more rectangular one). At the same time, they are very similar, e.g., the bottom row and the rest of the screen have icons in a grid. Importantly, you can protect your design also with patents (e.g. the Coke bottle). So when you’d see a competitor coming out with a very similar look and feel you could assert your rights to the design invention.

This is precisely what happened in 2011 when Samsung came out with their response to the iPhone and iPad, and Apple sued them over patent infringement. In fact, they not only sued over strictly design but also over essential functional elements (e.g. the pinch-to-zoom functionality). The saga travelled around the world and was trialled from the US through Germany to South Korea and Australia. Shortly after the initial filing, Samsung countersued Apple to make the story not so one-sided, importantly, they didn’t sue on the basis of design but over wireless technology patents.

Indeed, the question is valid why the Samsung design language changed so much? Image from iphonemod.net

Headlines were full of updates; my favourite one was a quote from the UK Judge who said Samsung tablets were “not as cool” as iPads. Although Samsung made design changes to avoid the worst outcome of injunctions (i.e. sentence of stopping selling devices), the court in Germany banned the Galaxy Tab 10.1. Interestingly, in South Korea on the home turf of Samsung, the court gave rights to both companies. In 2012, the US case initially ended with $1.05 billion sentence. Luckily for Samsung, it was later reduced to $539 million by the court. The parties finally settled outside of court, and the actual amount remained confidential.

There are some crucial legacies of these proceedings. One is that today companies meticulously document design and UX research processes to build a strong case against accusations from competitors that they are infringing other’s designs. Another is that since then, design cross-licensing deals mushroomed industry-wide. Fun fact, the pinch-to-zoom functionality is still on Samsung devices because Apple just patented one specific way of doing that.

3. The Fine Line Between Brilliant and Trivial: Amazon v. Barnes & Noble

While you can patent a design, there are very valuable processes you cannot. In order to be an invention, it has to include some kind of technological innovation. Take the example of clicking on the “Buy now” button on Amazon. On other websites, you’d enter a funnel of filling out shipping details, adding payment method, verifying all details and clicking the send order button. I hate it, and to be honest, it happened several times that I just decided to abandon the whole purchase because it was so cumbersome to place my order. The brilliant idea of Amazon was that since address and payment rarely change for most users, they could just automatically add those securely to each order and so improve massively the likelihood that the user purchases a product. Great, isn’t it?

Indeed, it’s so great that the US patent office granted this patent to Amazon (a bit of a trivia knowledge that Jeff Bezos was among the inventors). Amazon made — presumably good money — not only from actually using it for its own marketplace but also from licensing it to other companies such as Apple. Of course, having such an essential technology patent meant that Amazon had to actively look out for infringements and enforce its rights, motivating others to license the technology.

One of the inventors of the 1-click patent. Image from Daniel Oberhaus

They were eagerly waiting for the patent granting because Barnes & Noble introduced an Express Lane solution similar to Amazon’s 1-click technology. So, right after the patent was granted in 1999, they sued Barnes & Noble. Barnes & Noble claimed that although the idea is great, it’s also trivial, that is, anyone who knows web programming would do precisely that if they were tasked with speeding up the process of placing the order. Interestingly, the 1-click process patent wasn’t even granted in many countries for similar reasons outside the US. Nevertheless, it didn’t convince the district court, and they ordered Barnes & Noble to stop using the technology.

So, they had to change their process to a two-click version, where you still had to verify the details with an additional click. However, Barnes & Noble didn’t want to give up the fight so easily and appealed, bringing the case to the Federal Circuit Court. That court was not convinced by the inventive value of Amazon’s patent and cited a previous system by Compuserve almost a decade before that allowed for a similar single-action experience. They didn’t invalidate the patent, but they allowed Barnes & Noble to use their technology again.

This didn’t mean the end of the story, though. Both parties knew Amazon could still appeal the decision, so they settled outside of court in 2002. The settlement details are not public, but it seems Barnes & Noble didn’t license Amazon’s technology in the end because they continued using their 2-click system.

The big lesson from this case is that it’s often difficult to tell retrospectively whether an invention was trivial or brilliant. Inventors typically approach this by conducting extensive research on past technologies and clearly defining how their solution is novel compared to those. Of course, having these essential technology patents in one’s portfolio is a critical strategic advantage.

4. The Patent War of Attrition: Nokia vs. Apple

Some essential technologies become even part of standards (e.g. 3G/4G/5G), and they will be labelled as standard-essential patents (SEP). The good thing with those patents is that — since they are part of the standards — every company must license them. This is a serious advantage to the patent holders, luckily not infinite, as they are also required to license it under Fair, Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND) terms. This is supposed to level the playing field and while in many cases it works, it can also lead to lengthy patent disputes.

That’s what happened in 2009 when Nokia sued Apple over infringement of 46 of their patents on GSM, Wi-Fi and touchscreen technologies. They demanded Apple to pay a royalty per iPhone as a contribution to the creation of these mobile standards. Apple was in deep trouble because Nokia had a portfolio of more than 40.000 patents and had a strong patent and IP protection strategy at the heart of their business. This is so much so that in 2023, Nokia’s patent portfolio generated more than $1 billion in revenue (page 30).

Nokia has a long history of telecommunications innovations amassing an astonishing amount of patents. Image from www.tuquesabesdeesto.com

Having so many patents is a very powerful asset because the sheer amount of patents can make a court case simply too costly and time-consuming for anybody. Because although the news picks up more the size of the fines or settlements, there are millions of dollars in costs, even for simple cases. Now, in the case of 40.000 patents, this is obviously much bigger. And that’s not even mentioning the time, in court proceedings, they not only read the patents of the parties but do a discovery process which extends to both extensive research in the literature and into investigation of company internal documents. This latter is necessary to prove whether an inventive process was really taking place.

The dispute expanded globally, and in Germany and the Netherlands, Nokia even managed to get the courts to order Apple to stop selling iPhones. Ultimately, Apple accepted defeat and settled outside of court for $2 billion and ongoing royalties (the exact terms remained confidential) in 2017.

The lesson of this case is that the size of a patent portfolio matters. Although Nokia was no longer dominating the smartphone markets, its strong patent strategy became a financial lifeline for the company. Patents grant 15–20 years of protection, so companies can still profit massively from their past innovations even if their present innovation declines.

5. Pricing Out the Competition: Google Inc. v. Art+Com

We have just touched upon the costs of intellectual property disputes. The next case shows a clever but very dark side of this story. In 1995, a German design firm Art+Com filed a patent to protect their spatial visualisation method, Terravision, which is very similar to the latter product of Google Earth.

Google Earth launched in 2001, and the Art+Com team was discussing with Google to license or sell their patent to them; however, once they agreed on the general terms, the communication suddenly dried up. Initially, there were afraid to go against Google on court but ultimately, they did file a lawsuit in 2014.

They were seeking $100 million in damages for infringing their patent. They claimed that Google copied their solution after a 1994 demo the future Google Earth founding team attended. Furthermore, that they didn’t only use the method but also copied the code from Art+Com. If you want more details, I suggest watching the quite (i.e. not entirely) accurate Netflix series about it: The Billion Dollar Code.

See a great mini-series about this story on Netflix: The Billion Dollar Code. Image from sefiks.com

Google’s legal team followed a clever and dark strategy. They hired high-profile key witnesses and provided the Art+Com legal team with an obscene amount of documentation of the Google inventive process, trying to cripple them both financially and effort-wise. They also found a project called SRI Terravision from the Stanford Research Institute that showed a similar method for flying over geographical scales. The Art+Com team claimed that the SRI Terravision solution lacked key elements of their patent, most notably the tree-like representation of different zoom levels. Both the jury was not convinced and, in the end, deemed Google didn’t infringe the patent and that Art+Com’s patent invalid due to this prior art.

There are two main bitter but essential lessons from this case. First, patent litigation can be costly, and in some cases, the current practice favours patent holders with large enough budgets. Second, inventors should be very careful when sharing information with anybody outside of the inventor team. Demos should be constantly under NDA before the patent is filed and early documentation of actions (emails, discussions etc.) serve crucial evidence in a legal fight.

6. The Longest Process in Instant Photography: Kodak v. Polaroid

Let’s see now another case which has a similar David vs. Goliath vibe to it but ends differently. Edwin Land, the founder of Polaroid, was one of the greatest inventors in history. His dream was to make instant photography reality and despite how simple these instant cameras look today the technology is highly complex and sophisticated. Just think about it: the shutter exposes the film to light, which should create a photo positive BUT the film shouldn’t be light sensitive anymore when seconds later it exits the camera.

Many consider Edwin Land the greatest inventor of the 20th century. Besides of instant photography inventions, he pioneered polarized sunglasses too. Image from brewminate.com

Land and his team worked on the technology for a span of 30+ years by the time when Kodak came out with their instant camera. Kodak knew a lot about Polaroid and in fact they worked together in the past as Kodak was producing the films for the Polaroid cameras. They were also aware of the patents that Land and his team held but thought that on the one hand they are not strong enough and on the other the size of Polaroid does not allow a lengthy fight which would be peanuts for the world’s largest photo company, Kodak.

Kodak also cleverly implemented some solutions in their EK6 camera that made it not a simple copy of the Polaroid cameras. Unfortunately, those details made the camera heavier, less usable and the pictures more fragile. Nevertheless, the strategy was to gain market from Polaroid with aggressive marketing and discounts.

Polaroid had a strong patent strategy though from its inception and went on fighting for 14-years. Ultimately, the jury ordered Kodak to pay $925 million in damages and stop selling instant cameras and films for the existing ones in the market. This left an estimated 16.5 million Kodak instant camera owners in trouble. This was a textbook example of a devastating defeat. Speaking of books, A Triumph of Genius provides a detailed and engaging recollection of the case. It was one of the most best books I’ve read last year.

7. The Local Inventor Bias: Chint vs. Schneider Electric

One interesting subplot of the Kodak v. Polaroid case was how much they strategized about when to submit the case and which patent court to file it in because, on one, Kodak was expected to have an advantage, while on the other, Polaroid was expected to have an advantage. The obvious question is how much this is only part of the story and how much it’s common knowledge in patent cases.

Let’s look at one outside the US patenting courts. In 2006, Chint sued Schneider Electric in China for infringing their patent on a circuit breaker solution. The court relatively quickly reached a verdict and, in 2007, ordered Schneider Electric to pay ¥330 million (~$50 million). Although this appears small relative to the previous verdicts but in China, this was the largest award at that time.

Schneider Electric tried to turn back the decision by invalidating Chint’s patent by citing earlier circuit breaker designs that do the same thing. However, the patent reexamination board upheld its validity because there was no previous use or sale of that mentioned technology in China. In the end, the parties settled outside of court in 2009, and Schneider Electric agreed to pay Chint $23 million.

Circuit breakers may look simple but even in relatively well established fields there is room for innovation. Image from wikipedia

One could easily say that looking at only local patents is unfair, but the situation is a bit more nuanced than that. There is no such thing as a global patent, the closest to that is the so-called Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) which provides a unified procedure for filing applications BUT the granting of a patent remains the exclusive responsibility of nations. National decisions are also independent, so they can make even opposite calls. For instance, the above-mentioned goldmine 1-click patent of Amazon was granted in the US but was rejected by the European Patent Office for lacking an inventive step (precisely the point Barnes & Noble made). Still, the US patent office and courts have the right to deem it otherwise (which they did).

The lesson from this is that when you file a patent in a country and want to enforce your rights, you should be aware of the local regulations, policies and history of patent disputes. Patents are not only legal constructs but they are the result of legal, cultural, political and economic contexts. This means not only national differences but even regional ones.

8. Meanwhile in Europe: Panasonic v. Oppo

In an interesting initiative, Europe is working on a patent office (UP) and court (UPC) system that would operate over most European countries, making filing and litigation both streamlined and cheaper. Although the UPC only started in mid 2023, it already showed signs of its effectiveness.

It recently closed the case Panasonic v. Oppo case, where the earlier claimed that the latter was infringing its standard-essential patent on 4G technology. Since it’s an SEP technology, Oppo must have used it to comply with the standard. Oppo’s defense was saying though that Panasonic didn’t provide a license under fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms (FRAND), which is a requirement for SEP patents.

The judges considered this argument but found that according to the evidence, Oppo didn’t engage in good-faith negotiations. They didn’t provide enough information on the products or their sales and were inconsistent about which jurisdictions they wanted to obtain a license. To use an everyday analogy, this is like going into the grocery store and saying, “I want to buy apples; tell me the price?” and then complaining that the staff said, “It depends on what kind of apples and how much”.

The awarded amount was not too significant (€250,000), but it came with — the much more important — order to stop selling their devices in Germany, France, Italy, Sweden and the Netherlands. It might not seem significant but previously there was no court that could have ordered such a thing in a list(!) of countries.

The parties ultimately settled shortly after. Still, the case shows a precedent of how the UPC — a supranational patent court — handles these quite delicate SEP disputes. It’s still too early to make far reaching conclusions, but it will be interesting to follow the further developments of the UPC to see how a world with more “global” patents would work.

9. Can You Infringe a Patent in Your Own Backyard? Bowman v. Monsanto

All the previous cases had one thing in common: they were disputes between companies when one launched a product infringing a patent. For a long time, I didn’t know that you can actually infringe a patent as an individual, too, at your home, without trying to sell anything. The next case is a good example of exactly this.

Vernon Bowman, a farmer in Indiana, purchased Roundup Ready soybean seeds produced by Monsanto each year for his season’s first crop. Roundup Ready is a genetically modified soybean that is resistant a glyphosate, a kind of herbicide used to kill weeds contaminating the crops. However, for the second, riskier late-season planting, he had the idea in 1999 to buy soybeans from a grain elevator where he suspected that there will be some Roundup Ready beans were mixed into it. He went home and planted the seeds, then applied glyphosate on them to kill all others except the resistant Roundup Ready plants. He then waited for them to grow and harvested their seeds to be used for replanting in the following year.

If you also wondered why it is called Roundup Ready, here is the answer. Image from bearmarkethealth.blogspot.com

You could rightfully ask: Why go through all this grain elevator nonsense when he could have just used the seeds from his own first Roundup Ready harvest and reached the same result? Well, not exactly. Monsanto sells the Roundup Ready seed with a limited-use license, which tells that farmers cannot use the harvested seeds for replanting. He reasoned though, that because he didn’t use his own seeds but bought the seeds from an elevator without any license he basically found a workaround for the Monsanto patent and license.

Monsanto didn’t think so, though. When they learned what he was doing, they told him that he was in fact infringing the patent and they sued Bowman in 2007. Bowman’s argument was that there are limitations to the patent holder’s right, they can set conditions how the license may be acquired but cannot control what the buyer will do with the product. And although the license controlled that the original owner of the Roundup Ready seeds cannot replant his or her own harvest, they also didn’t do that: they sold it to a grain elevator… from where the clever Bowman bought it and used it for planting but the license did not control that.

This is a neat argumentation. But what Bowman didn’t consider fully is that even though he could even plant those bought soybeans, what he cannot do is create additional patented soybeans without infringing the patent. Bowman’s argument was that he also didn’t create them as the plants were creating their crops themselves. This argument didn’t convince the court though, and they ruled in favor of Monsanto.

Of course, the case remained quite controversial not only because it was a David and Goliath story but also because it has shown that patent law has to be reflected upon and even sometimes revised as new technologies (e.g. self-replicating technologies) stretch the boundaries of what previously was possible.

10. HeLa Cells: Discovery v. Invention

The last case I’m bringing to you is also about the limits of what one can patent. The story is quite sad but brings some very important lessons from the frontiers of patenting.

In 1951, Henrietta Lacks, an African-American woman was diagnosed with cervical cancer in the John Hopkins Hospital in Baltimore, Maryland. The doctors treated her with radiotherapy and performed a cervical biopsy to sample tissue from her tumour, without her consent. At first, she responded well to the therapy but soon her health deteriorated and she died the same year.

The hospital’s researchers found though, that the cells show a remarkable property: they doubled every 20–24 hours in vitro, making them the first immortal cell line ever to discover. This meant that they could use them for scientific study in various applications. So much so, that HeLa cells are an integral part of every medical and biology lab today. They enabled the development of countless drugs and vaccines, for instance, thanks to the HeLa cells, we have a polio vaccine. They used them to develop treatments for various viral infections and cancers and to study genetics.

One of the few photos of Henrietta Lacks, whose cells are now fundamental to the discovery of so many life-saving medications. Image from medbunker.it.

This also means that these cells are the foundation of the multibillion-dollar industries of pharmaceutics and biotechnology. Still, when the writer Rebeca Skloot searched for the family she found that they live in poverty because they never saw any profits from this exceptional discovery. They tried numerous angles to receive at least some revenue from the astronomical value that their relative’s cells enabled. One of these was to consider whether the cell line could be patented.

Unfortunately, there are several obvious issues. For one, the patent should have been filed before the discovery was shared with the public. Second, a utility patent only provides protection for 20 years, and there is a 6-year statute of limitation for infringement in the US. So this means that even if they had filed a patent before it was disclosed to the public in 1951 they still had to file a civil action no later than 1977. Nobody explained patent law either to Henrietta Lacks or later to her family so they didn’t think about a patent in time.

Another question is whether the HeLa cells qualify for the definition of an invention. For a long time, the USPTO allowed these discoveries of naturally occurring cells and DNA segments. This happened in 1994, when Myriad Genetics filed a patent on a cancer susceptibility gene, the patent office granted it and then Myriad created a quite lucrative monopoly from this asset. It took almost 20 years until in 2010 the Association for Molecular Pathology tried to invalidate this patent. The court also agreed with them, at the end of the trial (2013) they concluded that discovery or even isolation of naturally occurring DNA sequences is not patent-eligible. The same holds for the HeLa cells.

The lesson from this is that inventions require some kind of inventor initiated change in things. E.g. large rock that you can use to hammer nails into a wood cannot be patented, if you attach that rock to a stick to create a stonage hammer that can. If you find that chewing a stick can help making your teeth clean that cannot, but if you take a piece of animal bone and weave hog bristles in it to create a toothbrush that can. And finally, if you figure out a new way to find the minimum of a linear function that can’t, however if you figure out a way how on a computer you can do this in a memory efficient way that can (btw this is known to be the first-ever software patent).

I hope you enjoyed my selection of the 10 most interesting patent law and infringement cases. For the sake of accessibility to a wider audience I simplified some complex legal and technical details but if you are interested click on the links and go down the rabbit hole. And if you are interested in working at a place where you can work on patent applications yourself, check out our open positions.

Ten Patent Cases That Formed History was originally published in Product Coalition on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

Ten Patent Cases That Shaped History

Beyond legalese, there is a clever system for intellectual property protection

Working together with researchers, engineers, and lawyers on inventions and patents is among my favourite activities in my job. Early on, I had the fortune of learning from some very clever inventors and patent attorneys how a patent is built up and gaining a good understanding of the patenting language. No, I still can’t say I speak legalese but there are inventions, patents and patent dispute cases that I admire for their striking examples of human ingenuity.

The cases we are going to cover:

· 1. Infringement Without a Product: NTP v. Research in Motion
· 2. Function or Fashion? Apple v. Samsung Electronics
· 3. The Fine Line Between Brilliant and Trivial: Amazon v. Barnes & Noble
· 4. The Patent War of Attrition: Nokia vs. Apple
· 5. Pricing Out the Competition: Google Inc. v. Art+Com
· 6. The Longest Process in Instant Photography: Kodak v. Polaroid
· 7. The Local Inventor Bias: Chint vs. Schneider Electric
· 8. Meanwhile in Europe: Panasonic v. Oppo
· 9. Can You Infringe a Patent in Your Own Backyard? Bowman v. Monsanto
· 10. HeLa Cells: Discovery v. Invention

1. Infringement Without a Product: NTP v. Research in Motion

I guess you haven’t heard about NTP, but if you are over 30, you know very Research in Motion (RIM), the producer of BlackBerry phones. If you don’t know what those were, then in short, in the 2000s, every business person was fidgeting with one while they were talking about how important people they were. NTP, though never produced phones, in fact they don’t have any business other than holding and enforcing the patent portfolio of the founder, Thomas Campana. Campana was a smart inventor and a good patent expert; he patented some key technology steps underlying telecommunications and then tried licensing these technologies to big firms like AT&T and RIM.

Since RIM didn’t buy the license, NTP sued them in 2001 for infringing five of their patents covering the push-email technology that was BlackBerry’s unique selling point. The stakes were high because the court could have stopped RIM from using the technology, which would have been a disaster for all 3.5 million users in the US.

An iPhone 4 and a Blackberry Q10 next to each other. While the world was turning towards iPhone at this time, I did everything to get a Q10 for myself. I loved it! Image from Karlis Dambrans

The situation was so severe that the US Department of Defense (another heavy BlackBerry user at the time) also intervened and told the court that stopping the use of technology would cripple national telecommunications. RIM also tried everything; they spent a fortune getting the best lawyers and reasoned that because the servers are in Canada, the US patent law doesn’t apply; but none of these helped.

The resolution was ultimately to settle outside of court in 2006, and RIM agreed to pay NTP $612.5 million for a perpetual license on their patents. This was the largest settlement to date at that point showing how even tech giants are vulnerable to smaller entities holding essential patents.

Companies like NTP are an integral component of the patenting world today. They show that patents protect not only a company’s products or revenue streams but also its inventions. While NTP managed the portfolio of its founder, many similar firms just buy patents from inventors or bankrupt companies. These latter types are often referred to as patent trolls. If you are interested in how effective these companies can be, check out VLSI Technology (LLC, not Inc) and Uniloc USA.

2. Function or Fashion? Apple v. Samsung Electronics

When you hold an Apple or a Samsung phone in your hand, their shape and layout have some characteristics that are unique to the brand e.g. Apple had the circle home key while Samsung had a more rectangular one). At the same time, they are very similar, e.g., the bottom row and the rest of the screen have icons in a grid. Importantly, you can protect your design also with patents (e.g. the Coke bottle). So when you’d see a competitor coming out with a very similar look and feel you could assert your rights to the design invention.

This is precisely what happened in 2011 when Samsung came out with their response to the iPhone and iPad, and Apple sued them over patent infringement. In fact, they not only sued over strictly design but also over essential functional elements (e.g. the pinch-to-zoom functionality). The saga travelled around the world and was trialled from the US through Germany to South Korea and Australia. Shortly after the initial filing, Samsung countersued Apple to make the story not so one-sided, importantly, they didn’t sue on the basis of design but over wireless technology patents.

Indeed, the question is valid why the Samsung design language changed so much? Image from iphonemod.net

Headlines were full of updates; my favourite one was a quote from the UK Judge who said Samsung tablets were “not as cool” as iPads. Although Samsung made design changes to avoid the worst outcome of injunctions (i.e. sentence of stopping selling devices), the court in Germany banned the Galaxy Tab 10.1. Interestingly, in South Korea on the home turf of Samsung, the court gave rights to both companies. In 2012, the US case initially ended with $1.05 billion sentence. Luckily for Samsung, it was later reduced to $539 million by the court. The parties finally settled outside of court, and the actual amount remained confidential.

There are some crucial legacies of these proceedings. One is that today companies meticulously document design and UX research processes to build a strong case against accusations from competitors that they are infringing other’s designs. Another is that since then, design cross-licensing deals mushroomed industry-wide. Fun fact, the pinch-to-zoom functionality is still on Samsung devices because Apple just patented one specific way of doing that.

3. The Fine Line Between Brilliant and Trivial: Amazon v. Barnes & Noble

While you can patent a design, there are very valuable processes you cannot. In order to be an invention, it has to include some kind of technological innovation. Take the example of clicking on the “Buy now” button on Amazon. On other websites, you’d enter a funnel of filling out shipping details, adding payment method, verifying all details and clicking the send order button. I hate it, and to be honest, it happened several times that I just decided to abandon the whole purchase because it was so cumbersome to place my order. The brilliant idea of Amazon was that since address and payment rarely change for most users, they could just automatically add those securely to each order and so improve massively the likelihood that the user purchases a product. Great, isn’t it?

Indeed, it’s so great that the US patent office granted this patent to Amazon (a bit of a trivia knowledge that Jeff Bezos was among the inventors). Amazon made — presumably good money — not only from actually using it for its own marketplace but also from licensing it to other companies such as Apple. Of course, having such an essential technology patent meant that Amazon had to actively look out for infringements and enforce its rights, motivating others to license the technology.

One of the inventors of the 1-click patent. Image from Daniel Oberhaus

They were eagerly waiting for the patent granting because Barnes & Noble introduced an Express Lane solution similar to Amazon’s 1-click technology. So, right after the patent was granted in 1999, they sued Barnes & Noble. Barnes & Noble claimed that although the idea is great, it’s also trivial, that is, anyone who knows web programming would do precisely that if they were tasked with speeding up the process of placing the order. Interestingly, the 1-click process patent wasn’t even granted in many countries for similar reasons outside the US. Nevertheless, it didn’t convince the district court, and they ordered Barnes & Noble to stop using the technology.

So, they had to change their process to a two-click version, where you still had to verify the details with an additional click. However, Barnes & Noble didn’t want to give up the fight so easily and appealed, bringing the case to the Federal Circuit Court. That court was not convinced by the inventive value of Amazon’s patent and cited a previous system by Compuserve almost a decade before that allowed for a similar single-action experience. They didn’t invalidate the patent, but they allowed Barnes & Noble to use their technology again.

This didn’t mean the end of the story, though. Both parties knew Amazon could still appeal the decision, so they settled outside of court in 2002. The settlement details are not public, but it seems Barnes & Noble didn’t license Amazon’s technology in the end because they continued using their 2-click system.

The big lesson from this case is that it’s often difficult to tell retrospectively whether an invention was trivial or brilliant. Inventors typically approach this by conducting extensive research on past technologies and clearly defining how their solution is novel compared to those. Of course, having these essential technology patents in one’s portfolio is a critical strategic advantage.

4. The Patent War of Attrition: Nokia vs. Apple

Some essential technologies become even part of standards (e.g. 3G/4G/5G), and they will be labelled as standard-essential patents (SEP). The good thing with those patents is that — since they are part of the standards — every company must license them. This is a serious advantage to the patent holders, luckily not infinite, as they are also required to license it under Fair, Reasonable and Non-Discriminatory (FRAND) terms. This is supposed to level the playing field and while in many cases it works, it can also lead to lengthy patent disputes.

That’s what happened in 2009 when Nokia sued Apple over infringement of 46 of their patents on GSM, Wi-Fi and touchscreen technologies. They demanded Apple to pay a royalty per iPhone as a contribution to the creation of these mobile standards. Apple was in deep trouble because Nokia had a portfolio of more than 40.000 patents and had a strong patent and IP protection strategy at the heart of their business. This is so much so that in 2023, Nokia’s patent portfolio generated more than $1 billion in revenue (page 30).

Nokia has a long history of telecommunications innovations amassing an astonishing amount of patents. Image from www.tuquesabesdeesto.com

Having so many patents is a very powerful asset because the sheer amount of patents can make a court case simply too costly and time-consuming for anybody. Because although the news picks up more the size of the fines or settlements, there are millions of dollars in costs, even for simple cases. Now, in the case of 40.000 patents, this is obviously much bigger. And that’s not even mentioning the time, in court proceedings, they not only read the patents of the parties but do a discovery process which extends to both extensive research in the literature and into investigation of company internal documents. This latter is necessary to prove whether an inventive process was really taking place.

The dispute expanded globally, and in Germany and the Netherlands, Nokia even managed to get the courts to order Apple to stop selling iPhones. Ultimately, Apple accepted defeat and settled outside of court for $2 billion and ongoing royalties (the exact terms remained confidential) in 2017.

The lesson of this case is that the size of a patent portfolio matters. Although Nokia was no longer dominating the smartphone markets, its strong patent strategy became a financial lifeline for the company. Patents grant 15–20 years of protection, so companies can still profit massively from their past innovations even if their present innovation declines.

5. Pricing Out the Competition: Google Inc. v. Art+Com

We have just touched upon the costs of intellectual property disputes. The next case shows a clever but very dark side of this story. In 1995, a German design firm Art+Com filed a patent to protect their spatial visualisation method, Terravision, which is very similar to the latter product of Google Earth.

Google Earth launched in 2001, and the Art+Com team was discussing with Google to license or sell their patent to them; however, once they agreed on the general terms, the communication suddenly dried up. Initially, there were afraid to go against Google on court but ultimately, they did file a lawsuit in 2014.

They were seeking $100 million in damages for infringing their patent. They claimed that Google copied their solution after a 1994 demo the future Google Earth founding team attended. Furthermore, that they didn’t only use the method but also copied the code from Art+Com. If you want more details, I suggest watching the quite (i.e. not entirely) accurate Netflix series about it: The Billion Dollar Code.

See a great mini-series about this story on Netflix: The Billion Dollar Code. Image from sefiks.com

Google’s legal team followed a clever and dark strategy. They hired high-profile key witnesses and provided the Art+Com legal team with an obscene amount of documentation of the Google inventive process, trying to cripple them both financially and effort-wise. They also found a project called SRI Terravision from the Stanford Research Institute that showed a similar method for flying over geographical scales. The Art+Com team claimed that the SRI Terravision solution lacked key elements of their patent, most notably the tree-like representation of different zoom levels. Both the jury was not convinced and, in the end, deemed Google didn’t infringe the patent and that Art+Com’s patent invalid due to this prior art.

There are two main bitter but essential lessons from this case. First, patent litigation can be costly, and in some cases, the current practice favours patent holders with large enough budgets. Second, inventors should be very careful when sharing information with anybody outside of the inventor team. Demos should be constantly under NDA before the patent is filed and early documentation of actions (emails, discussions etc.) serve crucial evidence in a legal fight.

6. The Longest Process in Instant Photography: Kodak v. Polaroid

Let’s see now another case which has a similar David vs. Goliath vibe to it but ends differently. Edwin Land, the founder of Polaroid, was one of the greatest inventors in history. His dream was to make instant photography reality and despite how simple these instant cameras look today the technology is highly complex and sophisticated. Just think about it: the shutter exposes the film to light, which should create a photo positive BUT the film shouldn’t be light sensitive anymore when seconds later it exits the camera.

Many consider Edwin Land the greatest inventor of the 20th century. Besides of instant photography inventions, he pioneered polarized sunglasses too. Image from brewminate.com

Land and his team worked on the technology for a span of 30+ years by the time when Kodak came out with their instant camera. Kodak knew a lot about Polaroid and in fact they worked together in the past as Kodak was producing the films for the Polaroid cameras. They were also aware of the patents that Land and his team held but thought that on the one hand they are not strong enough and on the other the size of Polaroid does not allow a lengthy fight which would be peanuts for the world’s largest photo company, Kodak.

Kodak also cleverly implemented some solutions in their EK6 camera that made it not a simple copy of the Polaroid cameras. Unfortunately, those details made the camera heavier, less usable and the pictures more fragile. Nevertheless, the strategy was to gain market from Polaroid with aggressive marketing and discounts.

Polaroid had a strong patent strategy though from its inception and went on fighting for 14-years. Ultimately, the jury ordered Kodak to pay $925 million in damages and stop selling instant cameras and films for the existing ones in the market. This left an estimated 16.5 million Kodak instant camera owners in trouble. This was a textbook example of a devastating defeat. Speaking of books, A Triumph of Genius provides a detailed and engaging recollection of the case. It was one of the most best books I’ve read last year.

7. The Local Inventor Bias: Chint vs. Schneider Electric

One interesting subplot of the Kodak v. Polaroid case was how much they strategized about when to submit the case and which patent court to file it in because, on one, Kodak was expected to have an advantage, while on the other, Polaroid was expected to have an advantage. The obvious question is how much this is only part of the story and how much it’s common knowledge in patent cases.

Let’s look at one outside the US patenting courts. In 2006, Chint sued Schneider Electric in China for infringing their patent on a circuit breaker solution. The court relatively quickly reached a verdict and, in 2007, ordered Schneider Electric to pay ¥330 million (~$50 million). Although this appears small relative to the previous verdicts but in China, this was the largest award at that time.

Schneider Electric tried to turn back the decision by invalidating Chint’s patent by citing earlier circuit breaker designs that do the same thing. However, the patent reexamination board upheld its validity because there was no previous use or sale of that mentioned technology in China. In the end, the parties settled outside of court in 2009, and Schneider Electric agreed to pay Chint $23 million.

Circuit breakers may look simple but even in relatively well established fields there is room for innovation. Image from wikipedia

One could easily say that looking at only local patents is unfair, but the situation is a bit more nuanced than that. There is no such thing as a global patent, the closest to that is the so-called Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) which provides a unified procedure for filing applications BUT the granting of a patent remains the exclusive responsibility of nations. National decisions are also independent, so they can make even opposite calls. For instance, the above-mentioned goldmine 1-click patent of Amazon was granted in the US but was rejected by the European Patent Office for lacking an inventive step (precisely the point Barnes & Noble made). Still, the US patent office and courts have the right to deem it otherwise (which they did).

The lesson from this is that when you file a patent in a country and want to enforce your rights, you should be aware of the local regulations, policies and history of patent disputes. Patents are not only legal constructs but they are the result of legal, cultural, political and economic contexts. This means not only national differences but even regional ones.

8. Meanwhile in Europe: Panasonic v. Oppo

In an interesting initiative, Europe is working on a patent office (UP) and court (UPC) system that would operate over most European countries, making filing and litigation both streamlined and cheaper. Although the UPC only started in mid 2023, it already showed signs of its effectiveness.

It recently closed the case Panasonic v. Oppo case, where the earlier claimed that the latter was infringing its standard-essential patent on 4G technology. Since it’s an SEP technology, Oppo must have used it to comply with the standard. Oppo’s defense was saying though that Panasonic didn’t provide a license under fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms (FRAND), which is a requirement for SEP patents.

The judges considered this argument but found that according to the evidence, Oppo didn’t engage in good-faith negotiations. They didn’t provide enough information on the products or their sales and were inconsistent about which jurisdictions they wanted to obtain a license. To use an everyday analogy, this is like going into the grocery store and saying, “I want to buy apples; tell me the price?” and then complaining that the staff said, “It depends on what kind of apples and how much”.

The awarded amount was not too significant (€250,000), but it came with — the much more important — order to stop selling their devices in Germany, France, Italy, Sweden and the Netherlands. It might not seem significant but previously there was no court that could have ordered such a thing in a list(!) of countries.

The parties ultimately settled shortly after. Still, the case shows a precedent of how the UPC — a supranational patent court — handles these quite delicate SEP disputes. It’s still too early to make far reaching conclusions, but it will be interesting to follow the further developments of the UPC to see how a world with more “global” patents would work.

9. Can You Infringe a Patent in Your Own Backyard? Bowman v. Monsanto

For a long time, I didn’t know that you can actually infringe a patent as an individual, too, at your home, without trying to sell anything. The next case is a good example of exactly this.

For the sake of accessibility to a wider audience I simplified some complex legal and technical details but if you are interested click on the links and go down the rabbit hole. items.title: I Vibe Coded a Game That Attracted 10k+ Players In a Single Weekend
items.author: Ágoston Török
items.description:
Here are the tough lessons I learned so you don’t make the same mistakes

When I started developing my first online game — CEO Simulator — as a hobby ‘vibe coding’ project, I had no idea that a few weeks later it would attract thousands of players in a single weekend. This surprise success taught me valuable and humbling lessons about game development, user engagement, and the power of AI-assisted coding. Here’s my journey and what I learned along the way.

The gameplay of an impossible game

Creating balanced gameplay was critical to the game’s success. I couldn’t outsource this to Generative AI, but I leaned on DeepSeek R1 and OpenAI o1 (both advanced reasoning-optimized AI models) to review iterations of my rulebook. These reasoning models acted as a second or third pair of virtual eyes, catching inconsistencies and awkward phrasing that I might’ve missed.

Balancing the gameplay was trickier. I wanted a mix of randomness and skill, avoiding any “degenerate” winning tactics that could break the experience. To test this, I built a reinforcement learning (RL) setup with Windsurf (an agentic IDE) and Claude 3.5 that played the game repeatedly, learning and improving over time. I could have set it up to only operate with the game's internal state and actions, but I opted for actual browser interactions. This helped me find some strange UX behaviors, though actual game mechanics insights were limited. I had to opt for traditional playtesting, which in turn brought precisely the kind of learning I sought.

“Love it. Good work.” — Carson, an investor

Reflecting on it, I see multiple reasons why RL didn’t really bring good insights. For one, I should have paid more attention to the design of the modules so that they’re more compatible with an RL paradigm. Those strange UX behaviors, e.g. meant that I often found the agent stuck in stupid interaction patterns. Second, RL can be very powerful, but the learning curve is typically steep, so unless there are obvious degenerate winning tactics, it will take quite some games until the agent figures out the rules. Third, we humans still have an edge when it comes to gaining insights from a few exposures, so I could learn a lot more from playtesting it myself around 200 times. I recorded the data from these games, and the analysis helped me tweak variables like hand size, meeting durations, and game length (14 days).

“The no-reward, stressful nature of the game suits well for a CEO game.” — Fahad, a founder and CEO

The final rules go against everything I learned about board games. The cards demand a lot of reading, and 77% of the games end in a loss. Even more, winning the game happened only in 3% of the cases, and the remaining 20% of “not lost” games were won by just surviving. I deliberately kept these challenging elements because they mirror real startup executive experiences. You have to quickly digest lots of information, and if you don’t play for the long run — recognizing when to manage technical debt and avoid burnout — you’ll likely fail.

Similarly, a core part of the gameplay was the introduction of overtime and its connection to mental health. This resonated especially well with previous or current founders and startuppers.

“Surprisingly accurate.” — Ramon, a co-founder and CTO

Designing a simulator with cards

Design was where AI truly shone. Inspired by the word simulation, I aimed for an 80s retro aesthetic with synth vibes and low-resolution graphics. This was an ideal loosely defined task for vibe coding with Windsurf; I’d describe the vague idea (e.g. “Something like an 80s style arcade game”), and it’d churn out assets that nailed the look. This was the place where I also enjoyed it when it went overboard. For instance, at one point while implementing my request, it proactively and unwantedly added a neon highlight to the message box. I was like, hmm, that’s interesting, looked at it for another 10 seconds and decided to keep it because it was so on point.

I also wanted to keep the game lightweight. Therefore, I opted for SVG files over PNGs for card backs, slashing the footprint, and limited myself to just two custom fonts. I tried initially to generate the SVGs also with Claude and it was in the ballpark but ended up designing it myself for the intended look. Music was also a part where AI couldn’t help much other than implementing the code that handles music.

Localization added another challenge. Here I mean not only translation, let me explain. When I shared the game with my brother in Hungary, he remarked that some US startup scene references (e.g. WeWork or Theranos) could also be replaced with local references (e.g. French in France). I could describe what I wanted Windsurf to do, and it translated in Turbo mode all the cards and UI elements to 7 languages, including local references. Vetting these localizations is still some place, though, where I’m still seeking feedback from players, especially in the Spanish and French versions.

“The aesthetics are awesome and I love the simplicity” — Alex, an engineer

I also added a tutorial that shows below or next to the object that you should click on next, with a text that explains that action (see image). I got pretty good feedback from early playtesters on how important the tutorial part is, so I recorded a walkthrough video that complements the step-by-step real-time tutorial. Importantly, although the game starts with a big link to the video, only 31% of the players clicked on the video. Watching a video is friction, so it's best if you can get started without that.

Code: the good, the bad, the ugly

“Vanilla javascript + html? That’s sick!” — Niels, an engineer

Thanks to generative AI, I didn’t need to rely on my knowledge. Normally, as a Python dev, I’d have leaned on Python tools — possible, but clunky for a web game. Instead, I picked the right stack for the job: React + Node.js + Vercel. Windsurf made this choice a vibe-coding dream for me. I could “discuss” a basic security audit of the solution and deployment was a breeze with Github Actions, MongoDB/Cosmos DB and Vercel. I didn’t become an expert on any of these frameworks, but I still could confidently use my architectural and design knowledge to identify parts that were designed suboptimally.

Speaking of which, there were lots of things designed suboptimally. Vibe coding suffers from some serious flaws:

  • Bias for local solutions
  • Training data bias
  • Half-baked ideas

Let’s see them one by one. Bias for local solutions means that unless you say it explicitly in the prompt (which is not vibe coding anymore), the LLM will implement a solution in one place. For instance, at one point, I noticed that it started to put all functionality in the GameView.js file rather than separating concerns for game display, state management and menu behavior, for instance. This led to a high degree of coupling, low separation of responsibilities, and inappropriate intimacy. It would be good if these were issues affecting only humans trying to work with smelly code, but I assess that LLMs are even worse at dealing with poorly written code.

Training data bias is another issue. See, LLMs are trained with the commit history of public repositories. This means that the “mistakes” we humans often make in a commit will be carried over to AI-generated code. One such thing is when a commit also contains some small additional changes on top of what the commit message describes. This in vibe coding means that when I said “Implement a Special Card class based on the Card base class”, the model not only touched the Special Card class code but also refactored the Card class. Aladeen. I didn’t like it this at all and ended up reviewing every proposal line by line before committing to see what I wanted and what was just the LLM being too eager. This means some slowdown but ultimately I was still much faster than coding it myself.

Finally, half-baked ideas. Probably the most obvious when you start thinking about what could go wrong with vibe coding. We see that understanding requirements (i.e. prompts) is difficult already when people put effort into phrasing them. Imagine what happens when, in vibe coding, you say “Make it better” instead. Mayhem. The solution for me was to implement quite some tests that verified the end-to-end behavior of the system (so not unit tests).

An unreal Sunday afternoon

Imagine the following: I’m in the playground with my son, and I get a notification from MongoDB that my database is soon reaching the quota limits. I didn’t even know I had limits at first; I had to think about where I used MongoDB. Also, I never thought that the stupid little game logs were going to be this big, so I rather suspected some kind of hacker activity. I had to learn the hard way -again- this forever lesson of scalability:

Small things that might not be an issue when done twice start to become THE issue when you do it ten thousand times

Still from the playground, I checked a few things and found that the game is trending on Hacker News. I looked at the comments and immediately saw some good feedback about usability issues, as well as issues with the database filling up (high scores not added). So we went home quickly and I started looking into what I could do:

  • I could start compressing data or removing superfluous fields
  • I could look into whether I’m saving maybe even several copies of the game logs, hence filling up the database so quickly
  • I could delete data to save time
  • I could just pay for a bigger plan to save even more time for debugging
  • I could switch to another provider with more free quota (I did this later, Cosmos DB provides 25GB for free)

I opted for the last option as that was the quickest and cleanest solution (although not the cheapest but you can always choose only 2). Side track, that you can’t update the plan without ~5 minutes downtime, luckily the game used buffering so this meant people could still score only eventual consistency will be a b*tch at one point.

I had some tough choices to make also after the database fix, do I do any functionality improvement while the game is trending? As I said, some people in the comments indicated usability issues:

  • Cards on mobile were hard to read (too small fonts), and this led to accidental clicks (33.8% of users)
  • The tutorial was distracting as it covered half of the screen (~100% of the users)
  • Selecting an already moved objective at the end of the round led to an infinite dice roll glitch (?% of users)

My thinking was the following: this is a unique opportunity to iterate and learn how to make the game better. I can let these flaws stay, but that’s risking that, due to more and more people joining the bug reporting, the game will stop trending. I can try to fix these flaws, but I may cause regression and worsen things. I had some tests, but with that test coverage and my adrenaline in the sky, this was a real risk. I opted to take it anyway as I was here to learn and iterate.

Although the tutorial affects more users, I opted for fixing the mobile usability first, as the tutorial could be switched off easily, while the mobile usability was a persistent issue. I pushed these changes while the number of players was only around 3000, and by the end of the day, these bug reports didn’t populate, and I gained another ~7000 players. 80% luck and 20% skill. I also engaged in the comment section on Hacker News to make sure people feel that their feedback is valuable and being addressed.

Although I’m still not proud of the codebase, it’s certainly telling that I was able to make these changes quite quickly with no regressions at all. For a long time, I have believed more in these real-life code quality measures (i.e. ease of change) than e.g. cyclomatic complexity.

5 Things I Wish I’d Done Differently

  1. Set up testing and CI pipeline early
    The flow of vibe coding fooled me, so I skipped a proper CI setup with test coverage — a rookie move. AI eagerly overhauled code beyond my prompts, forcing me to manually analyze diffs before commits. A test suite would’ve caught regressions faster, especially after two buggy prod releases. Next time, I’ll prioritize this from day one.
  2. Start using MCP sooner
    Still, to the testing topic, but at least here I have an excuse: It was added to Windsurf after I started the project. Nevertheless, once I set up MCP servers (most importantly for Puppeteer), the otherwise painstaking browser interaction testing tasks became fun.
  3. Learn earlier how to undo changes
    Reverting changes made by the AI is not entirely trivial in Windsurf. This is especially problematic when I’m pair coding in turbo mode. Learning about that revert button happened embarrassingly late in my case so I was double doing quite often changes because my go to method was git reset --hard .
  4. Scalability planning
    I underestimated traffic. Sound downloads and move-logging hit Vercel and MongoDB limits fast — 35,000+ games clogged the system, locking leaderboard access. I scrambled to optimize, but early planning (or paid tiers) would’ve kept it seamless. You know the saying “dress for the occasion”. Another part of this planning was that I realized a bit late that although the leaderboard is a good retention feature once it’s filled up by really great scores (e.g. one player played close to 3 hours once to land on the top) it’s not motivating anymore for the remaining 98% of players. Saving thought was the implementation of the daily leaderboard.
  5. More proper risk modelling
    Some people noticed that they can submit any score to leaderboard using the API call. I manually deleted fakes mid-viral spike before coding a proper server-side validation — lame oversight. The sad thing is that I had all sorts of validation to mitigate the probability of a successful DOS attack but I didn’t think of these much more likely types of misuses. All in all, one of the “hackers” was a colleague of mine who was quite happy to find this vulnerability so I guess the game brought fun in unintended ways too :)

Closing Thoughts

Vibe-coding CEO Simulator became an unexpected lesson in modern indie game development. What began as a personal experiment — combining my limited web development skills with AI assistance — evolved into something that resonated with thousands of players within days. The game’s topic, brutal difficulty and retro aesthetics resonated with people in ways I hadn’t anticipated.

The journey wasn’t smooth, yet watching the player count climb past 10,000 that first weekend was worth every bit of frustration during the development. Moreover, this experience has transformed how I think about building software. The traditional barriers between “knowing how to code” and “being able to create” dissolve. With the right tools and mindset, the distance between concept and execution is getting shorter and shorter — though the fundamentals of good engineering, testing, and user experience remain as crucial as ever.

For anyone considering their own AI-assisted side project: embrace the imperfections, plan for unexpected success, and remember that even with AI’s help, the vision and decisions that make something worth experiencing still come from you.

This story is published on Generative AI. Connect with us on LinkedIn and follow Zeniteq to stay in the loop with the latest AI stories.

Subscribe to our newsletter and YouTube channel to stay updated with the latest news and updates on generative AI. Let’s shape the future of AI together!
